segments.text: Welcome, Heather.
We have 10 minutes with you this morning,
and that includes time for questions. We'll hand it over to you now.
Cool. Thank you very much, and thank you for the opportunity.
And also thank you to your support staff who've given me a time that works with
some accessibility needs I have.
I'm speaking on behalf of Just Kai.
We're a Kiwi organization that
seeks to raise awareness of modern slavery and to identify and
promote food products with slavery supply chains.
And I'm really delighted that we've got to this point with the Modern Slavery Bill.
I first spoke to my MP back in 2019 saying that it'd be really helpful if New
Zealand had this kind of legislation, and it's just amazing that
we've got to this point.
Currently, I find that there's an awful lot of confusion in this area.
For example, not that long ago, I spoke to an ice cream company that has a $50
million turnover and asked about slavery in the supply chain of their
sugar, which is a high-risk product.
And they simply said that they source from a large New Zealand supplier,
so of course they'll be fine.
And I come across that a lot, medium-sized players just assuming the big
players must be doing the right thing.
And it's really hard to even work out if that's true.
In that particular case,
I contacted the supplier. They told me they were slave-free.
Everyone always tells me they're slave-free.
But they actually voluntarily gave me their reasons,
which on the one hand was unconvincing, and
I'm not recommending them, but on the other hand,
was really useful. And that's the kind of thing that this legislation makes
available. It makes available the sorts of information that
you can then make assessments based on.
And I feel like that would've been really helpful for me if there was such a report
around, and that is a company that's big enough that they would be needing
to report under this legislation.
It would also be handy for the company, the small company that I talked about,
because there's a thing that they can look at rather than just making assumptions.
In the work that we do identifying products
with slave-free supply chains, we make really extensive use of modern
slavery statements
from particularly California and the UK, and more recently Australia.
A lot of the high-risk products for slavery do
have consumer-facing certifications that we can point to the public to
look for, but a lot of our work is on fish.
Fish is really high risk for slavery,
and there's nothing consumers can look for.
It's a matter of talking to brands and companies and finding out what they're
doing.
And just a couple of examples,
there's a company called Hartz that has a bunch of pet food brands in New
Zealand, pretty sure all of which have fish in them.
And on their modern slavery statement, they talk about their auditing process for
their suppliers, and it's just really useful having that information.
Even if they don't have that kind of information, there's another
company that's the parent brand of the Hill's pet food.
Again, pet food has so much fish in it. It's kind of bizarre.
But the parent company of Hill's kind of said that they had
75% of their spend in high-risk geographies
is audited, and so then I can talk to them, and they do many
things other than pet food, but I can then ask, "Is the pet food included in that?"
It gives you information that you can work with, even if the information isn't
quite what you need.
But also for those reports to be useful, I said
in my submission that it would be really helpful if there was a requirement
for the companies when they report to talk about known
risks and actually what they're doing about them.
I've read a number of reports from overseas where they just basically say,
"Hey, there's no risk of slavery in our supply chain, so we don't need to do
anything." And I'm like, "You're buying cotton from Xinjiang.
You're buying tomatoes from Australia.
How can you not know that there's risk in your supply chain?" But they don't have
to know.
And
for that kind of thing, I myself make a lot of use of the US Department of Labor
list of goods produced by child labor and forced labor.
Who knows how long that list will keep on being produced, but if it is, that's the
kind of thing people need to be looking at rather than just making assumptions.
And that means the statements are much more meaningful.
It'd be helpful for the ice cream company that I talked about.
I also would hope that having realistic knowledge about these
risks would
make that knowledge
trickle down to smaller companies.
Just this past week I was talking to a tea company that do spiced chai
teas,
and it was just really clear they had no idea about
what the risks were in their supply chain.
They said they were doing excellent things. They were doing almost nothing.
And I think they were in total good faith. They just didn't understand.
And also hopefully also make the knowledge trickle down to consumers
as well, who currently I find are
almost always really keen to do something when they know about these sorts
of issues, but have no idea what's going on or how to tell or how to assess what
companies say. So that was all I had to say,
if there are any questions.
Thank you very much.
I do appreciate that. It doesn't appear that we have any questions from the
committee, but that doesn't
reflect the lack of interest. It's just a very comprehensive
submission. Oh, we do have one from Camilla. Oh.
Just very quickly, Heather, I just wanted to know, who was your MP that you
visited in 2019?
Debra Russell.
Oh, right. Okay.
Yeah, from Helen.
Well, you'll be pleased to know that she supported this bill coming through.
Yes, for sure.
And she's very personally supportive as well, so I'll make sure I pass that on.
Cool. Thank you. Great. Thanks so much, Heather.
We appreciate it, both your written and your oral submission.
Cool. Thank you very much.
Thank you.
We'll move on now to Selwyn
ColesSelwyn,
hopefully, there we go. Look at that. Very efficient.
Selwyn, we have you
for five minutes. That includes time for questions if there are from the
committee. We'll hand over to you now.
Kia ora koutou. Thank you for the opportunity to talk.
And also, Camilla, I saw you there.
Thank you in particular for all your work in this space. So I'm Selwyn.
I'm an academic at AUT, but doing this in my private
capacity.
My basic contention
is this, that first
there are serious challenges in terms of enacting
effective modern slavery legislation in Aotearoa, and that we need to be honest
about those.
The thesis which I did my master's on was kind of tracking
those challenges and then trying to understand those better.
But, at the same time, there are good enough reasons
to enact something like this bill,
and then in particular, to bring a laser focus to the statutory
review
in three years. So just to kind of start with the challenges.
So, my view remains that there are real and often underappreciated
challenges in terms of getting a scheme like this working effectively.
In particular, if our goal is to make it ensure it makes a difference to the
lives and the working conditions of exploited workers.
So three that we could kind of focus on.
So first, in terms of ensuring that we translate disclosure into due
diligence.
So the theory is that disclosure will create reputational pressures on entities to
up their game.
I think with New Zealand's comparatively small civil society,
it can make it hard for disclosures to be meaningfully scrutinized.
And add to that the current kind of political skepticism about corporate
disclosures, there's a risk that the regime
generates
kind of flashy reports and struggles to move into due diligence.
A second challenge is around translating transparency into leverage.
So even once entities become aware of the inevitable modern slavery
risks and their supply chains, there's a serious question which remains about
what we expect entities to do with it.
In particular, the challenge that even many of our biggest commercial entities
often have minimal influence, particularly over their global supply chains.
The third kind of related challenge is this risk that
entities
who realize they have risks but struggle on the leverage side
will be tempted to cut and run,
to drop risky suppliers which can, in many cases,
worsen the lives of exploited workers.
So I did note a few recommendations that particularly respond to those challenges.
For instance, around more selective targeted reporting, backed up by the
capacity-building efforts from a modern slavery commissioner.
Including more specific language, potentially around collective leverage,
responsible engagement,
and perhaps more clearly delineating, at least in the implementation
guidance between domestic and international exploitation.
However, despite all this,
yeah, Camilla, yeah, go for it.
Sorry, I was just wanting to get the first question in when you're finished.
No.
Have you finished now?
I'll wrap up quickly, and then I'll go to-
Yeah, go for it. We usually have questions at the end-
Cool. Sorry
... Selwyn, otherwise you'll never get back to your point.
Cool.
I would want to say that despite all these challenges,
I would really not want us to get caught up in the weeds,
and to decide not to progress as we have many times before.
So just to wrap up with kind of a couple of encouragements.
The first is to be honest about those challenges, and in particular, in the way
that the commission kind of reasons through the submissions and writes its
report to be as precise as possible about how we're
defining the problem and how we think this law will make a difference.
But second, not to squander the precious bipartisan support
this has, and instead to distinguish between short and medium-term
objectives.
So I do think the bill is more than good enough for now.
It brings regulatory alignment with Australia, meets expectations under our trade
agreements,
and starts the process of shifting norms across the New Zealand market.
I do think in the medium term, there are big challenges to address,
but that these only can be resolved in
practice rather than in theory. So I think only after three years
of real-life experience implementing the law will we be able
to meaningfully review and then improve on its effectiveness, which is why I think
the statutory review is so important. I'll leave it there, and then any questions.
Thank you.
Great, Selwyn. Thank you very much. So we have a question from Camilla Belich.
Thanks, Selwyn. Thank you very much for your submission and for your engagement on
this. I just wanted to,
just because you've mentioned in your submission the
Modern Slavery Commissioner-
Yeah
... and I just wanted to, because this is an idea that
obviously other jurisdictions have looked at, and I just wanted to kind of get from
your perspective. When I kind of proposed this in an earlier draft of the bill,
there was a bit of criticism as to-
Mm
... why do we need a separate reporting entity?
And so just because you are supportive of this, I just wondered if you
could respond to those people, in terms of why you think it's a
beneficial thing and why you've advocated for us to kind of include that or
strengthen that.
I think, in particular, with a small New Zealand market, we
need some kind of entity which is specifically
helping entities to move from creating reports
to feeling the external and internal pressures
to actually do due diligence. Otherwise, the risk is that we just get lots of
flashy reports.
But we need a commissioner who is ensuring there's meaningful accountability for
that, but that is also matched by
targeted
capacity-building efforts.
I think without a commissioner,
it'll be a lot harder to actually ensure
that entities are lifting their game meaningfully on human rights due diligence.
Yeah, in particular, I don't think New Zealand, compared to other jurisdictions,
hasPerhaps a big enough civil society to
ensure that disclosures are meaningfully
scrutinized and that companies are held accountable.
Yeah.
Thank you, Selwyn. Really appreciate your submission,
both orally and written. Thank you for your time.
And we'd like to thank the submitter. Thanks.
Next, we have Save the Children. I don't have the names
of who we've got, so hopefully you will make yourself known to
us.
Jacqui Sowry, lovely. Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, that
includes time for questions from the committee.
We'll take that your submission is read and please
highlight what you'd like us to be across.
Thank you. Kia ora. Tēnā koutou katoa.
Ko Jacqui Sowry toku ingoa. No Wairarapa ahau.
Child Rights Advocacy and Research Director for Save the Children New Zealand.
Thank you for the opportunity to present our oral submission on the Modern Slavery
Bill. Save the Children was founded in 1919 and is the world leading
independent organization for child rights and children.
We work in 120 countries to save and improve the lives of children around the
world, including here in New Zealand, where we were founded in 1947.
We work to combat modern slavery globally with a focus on eliminating child
labor, child marriage, children in domestic servitude or bonded labor,
and the sexual exploitation of children.
Save the Children supports the Modern Slavery Bill.
We acknowledge the government's commitment and cross-party support to introduce
legislation to strengthen the ability to combat the harmful and illegal
practices of modern slavery in law.
Modern slavery is a global issue and needs to be eliminated.
It is the view of Save the Children that the bill is essential to enable New
Zealand to meaningfully tackle this issue.
The implementation of this bill would further advance New Zealand's
commitment to eliminating modern slavery, including forced labor,
child labor, people smuggling and trafficking.
While many people often think of slavery in historical terms, it is shocking that
27.6 million people, men, women and children,
are deemed to be currently in forced labor.
Additionally, 63% of forced labor happens in the private economy
and generates a $236 billion profit, and it is
happening across the world. And it is shocking to have it acknowledged it's
happening here in Aotearoa, New Zealand, where it's estimated to impact more than
8,000 people.
Save the Children recommends that children are acknowledged as victims of
and/or vulnerable to modern slavery.
Recognizing and responding to the impact of modern slavery on the rights and
well-being of children is essential if we are to eliminate modern slavery.
Modern slavery is a crime and impacts children across the world.
Children who are victims of forced marriage, servitude, trafficking of persons and
exploitation, including sexual exploitation, are known to suffer
physical, mental and emotional abuse and harm.
They are prevented from enjoying their rights to a childhood under the Convention
on the Rights of the Child,
and they are prevented from being protected from all forms of
violence, protected from exploitation, prevented from
accessing their rights to education, to play, to join in groups, and to have a
decent standard of living to ensure that they all grow up and be healthy,
educated, protected and included.
Governments, including the New Zealand Government, have the responsibility and have
signed up to ensure the rights of all children in Aotearoa, New Zealand are
understood, respected and upheld.
Although child labor and exploitation may not have had the same prevalence in
New Zealand as in other countries, it does happen, and cases have been prosecuted
in our courts. It is critically important that children are recognized as
being impacted by and victims of modern slavery, and that the bill directly
recognizes children and makes provisions to support children who are
victims of modern slavery. In regard to the bill, we make three
recommendations. Provisions should be made in the legislation to recognize and make
provisions for children who are victims of modern slavery to ensure their
protection, recovery and rehabilitation.
Resourcing is allocated to ensure that victim support and recovery can be
effectively implemented, and the establishment of a specialist personal
body such as the Independent Anti-Slavery Commissioner, who will include a strong
focus on children within their broader mandate of effectively
combating modern slavery. That concludes our submission, and I'm happy
to take any questions.
Wonderful. Thank you. Just a quick question.
You mentioned the forced marriage aspect.
Mm-hmm.
We had heard from another submitter about that.
Do you feel that, because obviously, in this particular case,
and I know that the members are open-minded, it's sort of a focus on
corporate-
Mm-hmm
... perspective. Where do you see that sitting in this?
It's sort of slightly different and difficult to manage.
What are your thoughts?
I think that's part of the broader scope of eliminating modern slavery.
And sometimes there are connections in a corporate aspect when there is payments
and transactions to marriage taking place.
So it's not completely a separate issue.
I think people understanding the broader aspects of modern slavery
are critical that they include children often.
We're part of discussions around the National Action Plan
that the government is leading at the moment to prevent the trafficking of persons.
Often children are hidden within those discussions.
It's really focused primarily at the labor
issue, and we see that through people coming to New Zealand
seeking employment and they're being falsely lured here, or their employment
is not there and they're paying for jobs that don't exist.
So that is better understood than the implications that impact children
who might be being brought into the New Zealand through adoption
laws or
through
seemingly holiday visits and end up being
part of servitude or forced labor in some form.
And because New Zealand doesn't have a minimum age of work, and there
are reasons for that, being in school as a primary protection
toEnsuring children are not working.
We still need to recognize that as a loophole.
So if a child is not in school, not enrolled, and therefore not visible,
they've got greater vulnerabilities to the issue of modern slavery
and forced labor.
Thank you. Thanks. A question from Camilla Belich.
Thank you. And thanks for the work that you do and for your submission and for
supporting this bill. In terms of
child labor and child exploitation, it's obviously a
really serious issue, and I know, I think almost half of the people
involved in modern slavery are children, which is really concerning.
What does that look like to you in terms of on a practical
level? We've just had some questions around
the definitions of child exploitation and child labor and
just wanting to get your perspective as someone who's worked in that area of
what that looks like.
Well, I think there are some strong existing definitions.
So labor that is harmful, prevents children from having a
childhood, prevents them from those protections under the Convention on
the Rights of the Child. Often, I guess
reasoning is given that we don't have a minimum age of labor because children do a
paper run. But when we really look at the definitions of forced
labor or child labor, they're quite different.
A child doing an after-school paper run on their bike or walking,
they're still at school, they still have friends, they still play, they still
access healthcare. Their childhood is not being damaged or harmed.
It's an addition to their
part of life as a child, whereas a child of forced labor is unable to go to
school, barely able to go to school, is prevented from having friends and
playing. And that's what we see
internationally when we're working on this, that absence of childhood, that
children are not playing, that they're not joining in with their friends.
That if they do get to go to school, it's that absolute minimum, and they're
leaving early. And so a lot of the work we do is to ensure that children
and supporting families to ensure that their children can go to school, and
sometimes that means that we're providing economic supports to
families who can then send their child to school, rather than
having them at home working on farms or weaving carpets or whatever it may be.
Thank you.
Thank you very much. Thank you, Jackie.
Really appreciate your participation in the process and the written submission as
well. Very
comprehensive.
And we appreciate it, your time. We'll move on now to our next submitter.
Next, we have Peter McKenzie-Bridle.
Hopefully. There we go. Look at that.
Peter, we have five minutes with you this morning.
That includes time for questions, and we'll hand over to you now.
Thank you. And look, I appreciate the opportunity to present to the Select
Committee on this important bill.
I trust you've had the chance to read through my written
submissions, so I don't intend to read those to you or to
repeat those.
However, I just want to
summarize the key points of those before I wanted to take the
opportunity to answer questions and to respond to some points that have been raised
by other submitters that I've read through their written submissions.
The key points
of my submission are to emphasize the importance of due diligence,
and that due diligence is an obligation
that needs to be imposed upon reporting entities in this bill.
The absence of an obligation to undertake due
diligence, in my submission, is a
serious omission in the bill.
There is a very good definition of due diligence.
Due diligence is an important aspect to ensuring that
the scourge of
modern slavery is removed from our supply chains.
But the reporting only on
activities that have
been undertaken to
achieve that objective,
the experience that I've been able to review in
other jurisdictions is that reporting only does not achieve
that objective. That reporting is merely, and this is a risk
for the New Zealand regime, a box-ticking exercise
that becomes akin to greenwashing, where the
actual substance of the issue is not
affected by the reporting regime.
There might be comfort at one level that there's visibility on these
important issues and that those
issues are being addressed. But actually on the ground, there's not really a
material effect or a change that's occurring.
So in my view, it's really important, it's essential
actually, that due diligence is an obligation that's imposed on reporting entities,
that they actually undertake and complete due diligence.
That would be strengthened, supported also by strengthening the role of the
commissioner. The bill at the moment proposes a commissioner,
says a commissioner may be appointed,
and that is inadequate, in my submission.
That the commissioner's role is a really
important corollary to the other obligations that would be imposed in the
bill to ensure that
the steps that are required to remove slavery from supply chains actually
occur.
And then thirdly,
in promoting
the ability of civil society organizations
to bring about accountability for
reporting entities to
ensure that those obligations are being upheld.
Some members may have a concern about that, but
there's a
good example of how
a similar level of accountability occurs in health and safety at work
area, where private prosecutions can be brought in respect of health and safety at
work breaches.
The WorkSafe can bring
proceedings against a potential or alleged
breacher-... is
itself, but there's an ability in those extreme
examples,
or really clearly merit-worthy examples where
an individual who has an interest, a legitimate interest in, or an
organization, sorry, that has a legitimate interest in the objective of the bill
can then pursue that accountability through the courts with leave of the
court to be able to do that.
So those are the principal points in my
submissions.
In terms of
other submitters,
I was particularly concerned by the Commonwealth Bank of Australia's
suggestion that New Zealand aligns with the Australian regime.
The Australian regime is problematic.
The
Australian regime, and I've indicated this in my
submission, was roundly criticized shortly after it was brought in for having
only a reporting-only regime, which is what New Zealand is proposing to
do.
That regime was reviewed in 2023
by Professor John McMillan, and
that lack of enforceability around reporting
obligations was a serious omission in the Australian regime
that Professor McMillan had identified, and that's the risk I believe that New
Zealand is facing here with this bill is that without
a
enforceable obligation,
the regime would be as ineffective as the Australian regime
has been in dealing with those issues.
Fletcher Building also has submitted that New Zealand should align with
broader international developments, including in the UK and the European Union.
That is something I think that the committee should certainly
endorse. But those jurisdictions are actually strengthening and
extending due diligence obligations, not weakening them.
And
if New Zealand were to follow that submission
as urged by Fletcher Building, there would be the imposition of a due diligence
obligation.
So those are my submissions. Oh, sorry, the last point was that
I was looking for the Business New Zealand submission to review that
because Export New Zealand, for example, and
the Motor Industry Association had both referred to Business New Zealand's
submission, but that hasn't been published, I believe, on
the Parliament's website, and I'm not sure what the contents of that
submission say.
If this is a public process, that's a submission that should be
publicly available, so it's available for scrutiny.
So those are my submissions.
Thank you.
I'll take any questions.
Thank you, Peter. We'll look into the Business New Zealand submission because all
submissions should be publicly available.
They've been tabled and released, so it may be that it's an issue there,
but we'll look into that. Thank you.
It doesn't appear that we have any questions, but that is not a reflection of a
lack of interest. It is just that you have given us a really comprehensive
and well-thought-out submission.
So thank you so much for being part of this process.
Thank you for your time.
Thank you, Peter. We'll move on now to our next submitter, which is Retail New
Zealand.
And Retail New Zealand, we have got by the looks of it
Carolyn Young with us. Great.
Good morning, Carolyn. And we have-
Good morning. Good morning
... as usual to see you.
Nice to see you.
Yes, again.
Frequent flyer with us. We have 10 minutes with you, as you know.
Includes time for questions, and we'll hand over to you now.
Yeah, I'm running solo today, and the point person on the
Modern Slavery Bill is Denise actually, and she's on holiday.
So I'll do the best I can. I have had some involvement, but you'll have obviously
read our submission. I've got a few things I'm going to read through just to
highlight some areas that we have.
So look, we want to start by saying that Retail NZ and our members strongly
support the intent of the bill to strengthen protections against modern slavery,
such as forced labor, human trafficking and exploitation, as well as
improving accountability and transparency within the business operations and
supply chain.
Look, there's several parts of the legislation that we think need to be
strengthened and considered.
My understanding is this is a member bill.
We have-
Yep
... had some experience with a member bill that has passed
through Parliament and been enacted, and then there's been
some
difficult challenges around interpretation for the ministry that
is then regulating. And once you have
a member bill that's passed all the way through, the only way to correct the errors
is to actually
go back and make changes. So we would strongly recommend that with the
member bill, that whichever, whether it's Justice or
whoever's going to be regulating, I'm assuming it's Justice, that they actually
do some work before it gets passed through, so that it's
really clear some of the intent, because
nothing against a member that's putting it up, but actually the way in which it's
going to be interpreted is really important.
So we would recommend that in the first instance.
We also believe that it would be really useful to be a fast follower
and to be looking at the Australian model and looking at
what we can enhance and copy rather than
trying to jump ahead and go really, really quickly.
This is a significant piece of work for businesses to undertake, and to get
it right is going to be really important.
We think being able to evolve the legislation over
time, rather than try to do everything at once is really
useful. In a retail setting, the supply chain is really
complex for some businesses, especially those businesses with over $100
million turnover.
There are some really significant businesses that have got very high turnover, and
their supply chain is really extensive.
So the first things I just want to really pull out or tease out a little bit around
the
reporting obligations-We think that
having really strict reporting obligations in terms of the period that you report
on is not actually going to be workable for business.
Many businesses have different reporting financial years.
I've been a chief executive in three different businesses.
I had a 30 November reporting period, I've had a
31 December reporting period, and a 31st of March reporting period.
When you have different reporting periods to the accountability to government,
it's very hard to report for your financial
period and report for a second separate set of accounts.
So we're recommending that businesses should be able to report for their financial
period and provide that information within six months of their end of
financial year, that they stipulate their financial period, and they stick
to it. So we think that that's really important.
And we do think
that we should be aligned closely to the Australian model.
There's many Australasian businesses in New Zealand that
big brother is usually Australia.
Having aligned compliance and aligned
reporting statements would be really useful and we would
get more traction as we move forward.
We think that would be really beneficial for--
And that we could accept any statements made
by Australian companies could be the same replicated within the New Zealand
environment.
There is a piece we've highlighted in paragraph nine about allowing any
two or more related entities within the same corporate
group to submit a joint slavery statement.
A number of businesses will own more than one
retail entity, and it can become really complex to
separate out. They might be using the same supply chains and
all of those sorts of things for the different products that they have.
Would be really useful to be able to consolidate those, and we would ask that this
is considered.
Then if you think a little bit about businesses that are on the threshold of the
$100 million turnover, if they drop down to $80
million, do they then stop reporting?
Would you consider looking at a two- or three-year average period to ensure
that you're saying over any two-year period your average
turnover is over 100 million would make more sense to have.
And it might be a longer period, I'm not sure, but it's something that should be
considered because some businesses will flip in and out in terms of
that space, and then you'll get a lack of consistency in terms of
reporting and the work that needs to be done.
Then we just have some concerns around making sure that the reporting is
risk-based and proportionate to the seriousness of the incident or
complaint. That we focus on material instances that are
substantiated claims, they are key risk areas and systems used
to identify and investigate. So making sure that they're not suppositions,
but they're actually fact-based and we know that they've occurred,
versus reporting everything that's a possible.
If you think about the Privacy
Act, you report an actual breach, you don't report a suspected
breach. You have to investigate it, and if you don't breach it, you don't have to
report anything. So I think thinking about those sorts of things.
Then just thinking a little
bit about
how consolidated revenue would work for different
retail models, like a franchise model or a cooperative model, making
sure we're really clear that in a cooperative or a
franchise model, the businesses that run the
local retail store, they run it themselves.
That they are independently responsible, not the group
structure. So making sure that the legislation is worded really
clearly and articulately to make sure that we're not wrapping
up directors of a
franchisor that don't control what
a franchisee is actually doing, as an example, or in the cooperative model as well.
So we think that's a really important piece.
There's businesses like Shein and Temu that,
how are they going to be compliant?
How do we know that they meet the standards that we would
expect in New Zealand? And we think that's something that really should be
considered and addressed as to how that will continue through.
And I think just
before any further progress, I think the committees would be really worthwhile,
and we can arrange for someone to understand the complexities of supply
chain. Supply chain is really, really complex and so,
depending on the size of the business, you might be buying from a

The legislation in Australia was not producing meaningful

shape how we think about this bill.

and in the rural
workers. And I saw, I suppose this was one of the ways it allowed
MPs and the public at large to engage with
survivors of modern slavery and to understand really that experience, particularly
in a domestic context. And from a human rights perspective, it's really
important that
work that has been done at a parliamentary level incorporates
survivor voice.
And I think it provided other benefits from the educational and
deepening understanding perspective for MPs.
And I think what he said to us is both himself, practitioners, but
also MPs across the sort of House had really appreciated
and enjoyed the involvement.
Thank you both. Really appreciate that
submission, both written and oral as well.
Really helpful to us working through this process going
forward.
We'll move on now to our next submitter, and I'm sure we'll be in touch soon, no
doubt.
Thank you both.
Thank you.
Thank you all.
We have our next submitter, Community Law Center Aotearoa,
Karen Hodgson and Dylan Nightingale.
Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you this morning.
That includes time for questions. We'll hand over to you now.
Tena koe, Madam Chairperson, and tena koutou members of the committee.
Thank you for this opportunity. I'm called Karen Hodgson.
Kia ora. Ko Dylan Nightingale
toku
ingoa. So over six years at Community Law Wellington and Hutt Valley,
I've represented over 80 clients in migrant exploitation cases.
And that frontline experience has led to me publishing a Borrin
Foundation research
this year on ways of holding exploiting employers more accountable.
And I've also set up a charity called Very Me, which has
free multilingual information on its website and a free
evidence-gathering app, including time recording tool based on GPS
data.Just
a
quick bit of
context around the cases that we see at
Community Law in Wellington and Hutt Valley.
So the committee will be aware that census data says that approximately 30%
of New Zealand's population is born overseas.
20% of our current population identifies as either Asian, Middle
Eastern, Latin American, or African.
And at any one time, there are approximately 600,000 migrants in New
Zealand.
MBIE's 2025 Employment Monitor found that a quarter of migrant
workers remain at risk of exploitation, and 15%
are described as knowledgeable but trapped.
So they know they're being exploited, but they cannot leave.
Complaints to MBIE have tripled from about 800 in
And 12% of those on work or student visas have reported being paid
below the minimum wage. But we know that that is just a fraction of
what is actually happening out there.
Nobody knows the true statistics as there is significant
under-reporting. The Ministerial Advisory Group on Transnational Serious and
Organized Crime, in a report last year, said that there are almost
certainly exploited migrants in our horticulture, construction, beauty services,
and hospitality sectors, and those are the sectors that
we see most often in terms of the clients that come to us.
So exploitation
starts as visa dependency, immigration coercion, excessive
hours, underpayment, and it escalates from there.
If we wait until it looks like modern slavery, we have waited too
long. And I'd like to give you an example
of
a real case.
So a migrant worker who was on an accredited employer work visa was working
in a convenience store in a small rural town.
They paid a premium offshore to secure that job.
This was money borrowed from family back home, and money that they were
counting on being able to repay once they were earning New Zealand
wages. When they arrived, the reality was nothing like what they had been
promised. The roster said 35 hours a week.
They were working 90 hours from 8:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m.
seven days.
Every week, they were required to go to a local ATM and take
out $300 in cash and give that back to their employer.
The time sheets were false, and the underpayment was severe and systematic.
They wanted to leave, but they couldn't. The visa was tied to the employer.
They were in debt from the premium they had paid, and they
had no realistic way to repay what they owed if they walked out.
So this is not someone being held in chains, but it is someone who is
completely trapped, and that is modern slavery.
So what did people see around them?
Well, a bread delivery driver came to that store most evenings.
A milk delivery driver came most mornings, and fruit and veggie
suppliers dropped off stock regularly.
They saw the same person there every single time they came,
early morning till evening, seven days a week.
So under this bill, does any of that trigger anything?
Well, in one scenario, absolutely nothing.
The delivery drivers might simply think that that person works a lot of hours.
And even if they are aware of modern slavery law, they may think, "Well, this
person isn't a slave. They're not being held against their will.
They're just standing behind a counter. They seem fine.
This is an employment issue. It is not modern slavery."
So that instinct to file this away as just an employment issue
is a critical gap that this bill needs to close.
Because that dismissal is exactly what this bill's high threshold enables.
If people only look for chains and locked doors, everything else goes
unchecked. Exploitation presents as patterns.
The same person, never a day off, no breaks.
These are all invisible indicators.
The problem is not that people are not seeing them.
The problem is that without clear guidance, people don't know what they are
seeing or feel they can't act unless they're certain it meets some
legal threshold they don't fully understand.
This bill presents an important opportunity to change that, not just
through reporting obligations, but through a public awareness campaign that
teaches people to recognize the full spectrum and tells them
clearly, "You don't need to be certain it's modern slavery.
If something doesn't look right, say something."
The delivery drivers, the customers, the neighboring businesses, they are all
potential eyes and ears, but only if the bill and the awareness
campaign around it equips everyone to act.
Because this kind of exploitation is happening in every community in New Zealand,
in our takeaway shops, beauty salons, construction sites, orchards, bars, and
cafes. It is not rare, and it is not hidden.
It is just not being recognized. And as Karen said, this
needs to be victim safe. So if those delivery drivers report
their concerns to their managers, what happens next?
Well, under the bill as currently drafted, that is not well worked through.
And for this worker, a poorly managed report could be catastrophic.
Their employer finding out that they have been reported could put them at serious
risk, not only of jeopardizing their visa, but they could face
pressure, threats, and worse.
So any information that is reported must be handled with the victim's safety at the
center, and that means before sensitive information reaches
anyone who could identify the worker, their safety must be assessed
and secured.
People who are approaching that worker must be trained to do that
in a way that does not expose them to further harm.
The worker must be presented with options in their own language and plain terms.
They need to know, if you want to leave, here is how you will be
supported. Here is what a protection visa looks like, and here is
who will help you find somewhere to stay and another
job.Communications from MB must clearly
state that workers will not be penalized for visa breaches that result from
exploitation.
There is a migrant exploitation protection visa.
It's only for six months, and that is not long enough to find alternative
employment, stabilize, and rebuild after trauma.
It should be at least 12 months, which is what it originally was.
We also need more community-based navigation services, trusted
people embedded in communities who can reach that store worker
and support them.
Legal aid should be extended to cover MEPB applications.
So all of this has to be thought through very carefully.
One of the biggest barriers I've documented is evidence.
In this situation, as in most others I have worked in, the
employer holds all the records and they
are all false. They're fabricated after the event,
and it becomes very difficult for the worker to prove
the wage theft that has taken place.
So just bringing it back to the bill.
So as Karen said,
in our submission, we talk about
some definitional issues we've identified and due diligence gaps.
So the convenience store worker situation, the systemic
underpayment over months,
false time sheets, and cash paybacks from wages
would likely constitute a serious violation of legislation relating to
employee rights and therefore would be captured by the
bill's definition of forced or exploitative labor.
So that worker is probably in scope, but there are still risks in not
explicitly linking the definition to sections
351 and 351A of the Immigration Act.
So those sections, in our submission, should be referenced in the
definition of modern slavery in paragraph A.
And then the bill's serious violation
threshold
has no guidance.
And without it, reporting entities will make their own judgment calls,
and it is quite likely they will set that bar too high.
And finally,
yes, so even where conduct such as the store
worker, it might technically be captured,
not naming sections 351 and 351A of the
Immigration Act signals to reporting entities that
immigration-based coercion is peripheral to modern
slavery. If it is not named, it will not be looked
for.
And finally, on due diligence,
a supplier to that convenience store may be required to
report on its supply chain, but reporting is not the
same as investigating it.
So without a mandatory due diligence obligation, an entity can look superficially,
find no clearly labeled instance, and be satisfied that the bill's
requirements are met. The law should not simply
accept that an entity does not know.
It should ask, "Did you take reasonable steps to find out?" That is a very
different obligation, and right now the bill does not require it. Bye.
Thank you both for your submission.
We've gone well over time with you this morning, but really appreciate how
comprehensive your submission has been for us.
Really helpful for our process, so thank you.
Thank you.
Committee, we're going to move into full committee.
You've got the link for that.
The time for that is already listed.
So we'll see you in there
about 10 minutes.
